source.title: Diversity is All You Need: Learning Skills without a Reward Function

confidence_rationale: ICLR publication; strong theoretical result (unique optimum = even state-space partition); multiple robotic benchmarks confirm diverse skill emergence. Mapping fidelity is high for ARC-065 (behavioral diversity generation). The REE analog -- using mutual information between context and visited states to prevent mode collapse -- is structurally identical to DIAYN's objective. Main caveat: DIAYN operates in pre-training (no task reward); REE needs diversity during task learning, which is harder.
failure_signatures: Skills may fail to scale to very high-dimensional observation spaces, suggesting MI-based diversity objectives need a compact behavioral embedding (not raw observations) to work in REE's latent space.

Some behaviors remain suboptimal even after training (e.g., ant forward locomotion). DIAYN can learn orthogonal skills that are individually suboptimal rather than the dominant-plus-diverse structure REE needs.

DIAYN requires domain knowledge to incorporate priors about task-relevant features -- without this, the diversity objective may reward modes that are irrelevant to the task (undirected diversity problem).

mapping.source_claim_statement: Maximizing mutual information I(S;Z) between a discrete skill variable Z and visited states S, under a maximum-entropy policy, forces the policy family to partition state space evenly across skills, preventing collapse to a single mode. The unique optimum has each skill uniformly distributed over its own state partition.
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-065 (behavioral diversity generation) is partially implemented by MECH-314 (novelty/curiosity bias in E3 scoring). DIAYN's theoretical result says that if REE's diversity objective can be framed as maximizing MI between a context variable (which region/goal-mode is active) and the states visited under that context, gradient-based training will structurally prevent monostrategy. This maps to REE needing a context-conditional discriminability term in the E3 scoring function, not just a novelty penalty on individual steps.
mapping.mapping_caveat: DIAYN operates fully unsupervised (no task reward). REE trains with concurrent task reward (harm avoidance + goal pursuit). Multi-objective tension means the MI diversity term will compete with the forward model F which dominates E3 score variance (EXQ-571 finding: F variance fraction ~0.89). The DIAYN solution -- using a separate diversity-only training phase before task training -- may be more tractable than adding diversity bias to a live task-reward pipeline.
mapping.source_context: Simulated robotics (MuJoCo: HalfCheetah, Hopper, Ant); discrete skill variable Z with 50 skills; SAC policy optimizer; pre-training without task reward.

confidence_components.notes: ICLR is a top-tier venue. Theory is clean. Transfer risk moderate: REE has concurrent task reward, not a pure diversity-training phase; and REE's monostrategy is a route-preference problem in a navigation domain, not a locomotion skill-diversity problem.
